Antimicrobial Resistance Genes

AMR Gene Analysis

Each AMR gene is shown per database with all genomes that contain it. Frequency displayed as count (percentage%). Critical resistance genes are highlighted.

Role of each gene family:
• Carbapenemases (KPC, NDM, OXA) – confer resistance to carbapenems, last‑line antibiotics.
• ESBLs (CTX‑M, SHV, TEM) – hydrolyze extended‑spectrum cephalosporins.
• AmpC – broad‑spectrum β‑lactamases, often plasmid‑borne.
• Colistin Resistance (mcr, pmr, lpx) – resistance to polymyxins, last‑resort for MDR.
• Tigecycline Resistance (tetX, efflux pumps) – resistance to tigecycline, an important option.
• Biofilm Formation (ompA, csu, bfm) – promotes persistence and device‑related infections.
• Efflux Pumps (ade, acr, mex) – multidrug efflux, contributes to MDR.
• Other – includes aminoglycosides, fluoroquinolones, sulfonamides, etc.

Virulence Genes

Virulence Gene Analysis

Each virulence gene is shown per database with all genomes that contain it. Use the filter buttons below to focus on specific virulence families.

Role of each virulence family:
• ybt – Yersiniabactin, siderophore for iron acquisition, linked to ICEKp.
• clb – Colibactin, genotoxin involved in DNA damage.
• iro – Salmochelin, siderophore for iron scavenging.
• iuc – Aerobactin, siderophore associated with hypervirulence.
• rmp – Regulator of mucoid phenotype, hypermucoviscosity.
• fim – Type 1 fimbriae, adherence to host surfaces.
• mrk – Type 3 fimbriae, biofilm formation and adhesion.
• ecp – E. coli common pilus, adherence.
• tss – Type VI secretion system, interbacterial competition and host interaction.
• siderophore – General iron‑scavenging systems.
• adhesin – Surface factors promoting attachment.
• toxin – Cytotoxins and hemolysins.
GeneCategoryDatabaseFrequencyGenomes

AI Assistant Guide

How to Use AI with This Report

You can upload this HTML report (or its JSON data) to AI tools like ChatGPT, Claude, or Gemini to ask detailed questions about your K. pneumoniae dataset. Below are example prompts to get you started.

General Questions

  • What are the most common STs in this dataset?
  • Which K and O capsule types are dominant?
  • How many samples carry carbapenemase genes? Which ones?
  • Are there any samples with both colistin resistance (mcr) and carbapenemases?
  • What is the overall quality of the assemblies (N50, total length, GC%)?

Virulence Analysis

  • Which virulence genes are most prevalent in hypervirulent clones (ST23, ST86)?
  • List all samples carrying the ICEKp markers (ybt, clb, iro, rmp).
  • Which samples contain the aerobactin (iuc) system? Are they associated with specific STs?
  • Show me the co-occurrence of rmpA and iuc in the same genome.
  • Which samples carry fim, mrk, or ecp adhesins?
  • Identify isolates with Type VI secretion system (T6SS) genes.

Resistance Patterns

  • What is the prevalence of blaKPC, blaNDM, and blaOXA-48-like genes?
  • Which STs are most associated with carbapenem resistance?
  • Are there samples with high-level aminoglycoside resistance (armA, rmtB)?
  • Which resistance genes co-occur most frequently? Provide examples.

Pattern Discovery

  • Identify samples with high‑risk combinations (critical resistance + hypervirulence).
  • Which ST‑K:O combinations are associated with MDR or hypervirulence?
  • Show me the distribution of ICEKp markers across different STs.

How to Upload Data

If you are using a text-based AI (like this one), you can copy the relevant tables or the JSON data and paste them into the conversation. For example, you can say: "I'm sending you the sample overview table from the Kleboscope report. Can you analyze the ST distribution?"

Alternatively, you can save the kleboscope_ultimate_report.json file and ask the AI to parse it. Many AI tools accept file uploads.

Export Data
